risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated randomization procedure with stratification/minimization variables, which indicates a proper random allocation sequence rather than a predictable or non-random method.

Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." Comment: This describes sequence generation and stratified assignment, but it does not state any concealment method (e.g., central allocation or opaque sealed envelopes). Therefore, the recruiter’s ability to foresee the next assignment is not reported, so allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS... All participants received twice weekly group sessions... delivered by instructors experienced in MT ... or certified in FFS respectively" and "The study was a randomized, controlled pilot trial" Comment: The intervention was a behavioral smoking-cessation program with two clearly different group treatments delivered by therapists, and the report does not describe blinding of participants or intervention personnel. Given the nature of the treatments, participants and providers would have known assignment, and knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence smoking-related outcomes (e.g., self-reported abstinence and cigarettes/day), so this domain is high risk.

Quote: "Self-reported smoking was assessed at in-person weekly visits by a research assistant who was not involved in treatment delivery via the Timeline Follow Back method (TLFB)... Self-reported abstinence was assessed using TLFB and verified by an exhaled carbon monoxide (CO) measurement..." Comment: The main smoking outcomes included patient self-report, and the patient is effectively the assessor for TLFB. The study does not report participant blinding, and smoking cessation/self-reported abstinence are subjective outcomes that can be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Although CO verification is objective, the worst-outcome rule applies because the self-reported smoking outcomes are vulnerable to detection bias, so the domain is judged high risk.

Quote: "The six, 12, and 17-week follow-up completion rates were 27 (82% of treatment-exposed individuals) and 33 (87%), 29 (88%) and 32 (84%), and 29 (88%) and 33 (87%) for MT and FFS respectively." Comment: Attrition was not trivial at all relevant follow-ups (e.g., 88% vs 84% at 12 weeks and 88% vs 87% at 17 weeks among treatment-exposed participants), but the more important issue is that the analysis used "intent-to-treat mixed effect regression" with "Incomplete data... handled using casewise deletion" rather than a clearly justified missing-data approach such as multiple imputation. For the smoking outcomes, this leaves the results vulnerable to bias from missing outcomes; in addition, the paper reports that 16 randomized participants "did not complete baseline paperwork and were not exposed to treatment," which further reduces the analyzed sample. Although the attrition is not above the strict 30% threshold, the paper does not provide a robust missing-data analysis and relies on casewise deletion, so the safest judgement is high risk for incomplete outcome data.
